# COMP110 Data Analysis: Peer Code Review Recommendation

## Project Overview

This data analysis project examines survey responses from 230 COMP110 students to evaluate whether implementing peer code review sessions would improve the course experience. Through statistical analysis and data visualization, I explored student demographics, academic backgrounds, and programming experience to determine if collaborative learning would benefit the class.

## Analysis Summary

I analyzed three key aspects of the student population:

**Major Distribution:** The class contains students from 30+ different majors including Biology (37 students), Neuroscience (33 students), Economics (22 students), and Environmental Science/Studies (22 students). This diversity suggests that students approach programming problems from different perspectives.

**Year Distribution:** Students span multiple years, with the majority being juniors and seniors (years 28-29) and a significant number of younger students (years 26-27). This creates natural mentorship opportunities.

**Prior Programming Experience:** Most students (63%) have minimal programming experience (less than one month), while smaller groups have varying levels of experience ranging from 2-6 months to over 2 years.

## Conclusions

Based on my data analysis, I **strongly recommend** implementing peer code review sessions in COMP110.

The data reveals a diverse student population perfect for collaborative learning:
- 30+ different majors bring varied problem-solving approaches
- Mix of class years creates mentorship opportunities
- Wide range of programming experience levels (63% beginners, others with months to years of experience)

**Recommendation:** Implement bi-weekly peer code review sessions where students work in small groups (3-4 people) to review each other's solutions. Start with a pilot mid-semester and adjust based on feedback.

**Benefits:**
- Students with little experience learn from more experienced peers
- Students with more experience deepen understanding by explaining concepts
- All students develop professional collaboration skills
- Reduced anxiety about finding "the one right answer"

**Trade-offs:** Requires 20-30 minutes of class time every two weeks, and some students who prefer working independently might initially resist.

The benefits of exposure to multiple coding styles, reduced coding anxiety, and development of professional skills far outweigh these minimal costs.
